questions.question: In a photoelectric experiment, increasing light intensity changes the number of emitted electrons but not their maximum kinetic energy. Which interpretation best fits this observation?
options: Intensity affects photon number, while maximum kinetic energy depends on photon frequency.
Intensity changes photon energy, while frequency mainly controls the number of emitted electrons.
Both intensity and frequency change only the work function of the metal.
The observation is impossible because more intense light must give each electron more kinetic energy.

questions.question: Assertion: Electrons can show diffraction in suitable experiments. Reason: Moving particles can be associated with de Broglie wavelength. Which option best evaluates this pair?
options: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and the Reason explains why electron diffraction is possible.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but the Reason applies only to photons.
Assertion is correct, but Reason is incorrect because wavelength is not associated with matter.
Assertion is incorrect, but Reason is correct only for particles at rest.
